note_data.title: Enzymes
keyPoints: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed
All enzymes are proteins made up of amino acids joined by peptide bonds
Enzymes work by lowering the activation energy required for reactions to occur
The active site is the specific region where substrate binds to the enzyme
Enzyme activity is affected by temperature, pH, enzyme concentration and substrate concentration
Optimum temperature for human enzymes is 37°C (body temperature)
Denaturation occurs when enzymes lose their shape due to high temperature or extreme pH
Lock and key model explains how enzymes are specific to their substrates
Cofactors and coenzymes help enzymes function properly
Enzyme inhibition can be competitive or non-competitive

definitions.definition: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up the rate of biochemical reactions without being consumed in the reaction.
The active site is a specific region on the enzyme where the substrate binds and the reaction takes place.
A substrate is the reactant molecule on which an enzyme acts to form products.
Denaturation is the process in which enzymes lose their natural shape and activity due to high temperature or extreme pH.
Cofactors are inorganic substances like metal ions that help enzymes to function properly.

importantQuestions.question: What is the effect of temperature on enzyme activity?
importantQuestions.answer: As temperature increases, enzyme activity increases up to optimum temperature (37°C for human enzymes). Beyond optimum temperature, enzymes denature and lose activity due to breakdown of protein structure.

importantQuestions.question: Explain the lock and key model of enzyme action. Also discuss factors affecting enzyme activity.
importantQuestions.answer: Lock and Key Model: Enzymes have specific active sites that are complementary in shape to their substrates, like a lock and key. Only specific substrate can bind to the active site forming enzyme-substrate complex, which then breaks down to form products and free enzyme. Factors affecting enzyme activity: (1) Temperature - increases activity up to optimum, then causes denaturation (2) pH - each enzyme has optimum pH, extreme pH denatures enzyme (3) Enzyme concentration - more enzymes mean faster reaction rate (4) Substrate concentration - increases rate until saturation point.

mcqs.question: The optimum temperature for human enzymes is:
The region where substrate binds to enzyme is called:

commonMistakes: Students confuse enzymes with hormones - remember enzymes are catalysts, hormones are chemical messengers
Writing that enzymes are consumed in reactions - they are NOT consumed, they are reused
Forgetting to mention that all enzymes are proteins in definitions
Not explaining that denaturation is irreversible process
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question in every BSEK paper
Always draw and label the lock and key model diagram - appears frequently
Memorize the exact definition of enzyme word-for-word from textbook
Temperature and pH graphs are common in long questions - practice drawing them
formulas: Enzyme + Substrate → Enzyme-Substrate Complex → Enzyme + Products
note_data.keyDiagram: Lock and Key Model: Draw enzyme with specific shaped active site, substrate fitting exactly into active site, enzyme-substrate complex formation, and finally products being released with enzyme unchanged. Label: Enzyme, Active Site, Substrate, Enzyme-Substrate Complex, Products.
